343	  \[
344	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
345	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
346	  \]
347	  which is a unit.
348	\end{lemma}
349	\begin{proof}
350	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
351	  \leanok
352	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
353	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
354	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
355	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
356	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
357	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
358	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
359	\end{proof}
360	
361	\begin{definition}
362	
363	\leanok
364	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
365	  \label{def:gr_transition}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
367	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
368	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
369	  % affine patches" (read from
370	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
373	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
374	  %  defined as follows. ...
375	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
376	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
377	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
378	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
379	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
380	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
381	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
382	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
383	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
384	  \[
385	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
386	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
387	  \]
388	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
389	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
390	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
391	\end{definition}
392	
393	\begin{lemma}
394	
395	\leanok
396	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
397	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
398	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
399	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
400	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
401	  % affine patches" (read from
402	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
403	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
404	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
405	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
406	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
407	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
408	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
409	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
410	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	\begin{proof}
413	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
414	  \leanok
415	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
416	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
417	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
418	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
419	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
420	\end{proof}
421	
422	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
423	
424	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
425	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
426	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
427	
428	\begin{lemma}
429	
430	\leanok
431	[Column submatrix of a product]
432	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
433	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
434	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
435	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
436	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
437	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
438	  the right factor:
439	  \[
440	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
441	  \]
442	\end{lemma}
443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
447	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
448	\end{proof}
449	
450	\begin{lemma}
451	
452	\leanok
453	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
454	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
456	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
457	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
458	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
459	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
460	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
461	  \[
462	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
463	  \]
464	\end{lemma}
465	\begin{proof}
466	
467	\leanok
468	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
469	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
470	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
471	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
478	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
480	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
481	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
482	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
483	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
484	\end{lemma}
485	\begin{proof}
486	
487	\leanok
488	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
489	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
490	\end{proof}
491	
492	\begin{lemma}
493	
494	\leanok
495	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
496	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
497	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
498	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
499	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
500	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
501	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
502	  \[
503	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
504	  \]
505	\end{lemma}
506	\begin{proof}
507	
508	\leanok
509	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
510	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
511	\end{proof}
512	
513	\begin{lemma}
514	
515	\leanok
516	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
517	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
519	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
520	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
521	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
522	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
523	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
524	  defining the chart.)
525	\end{lemma}
526	\begin{proof}
527	
528	\leanok
529	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
530	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
531	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
532	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
533	\end{proof}
534	
535	\begin{lemma}
536	
537	\leanok
538	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
539	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
541	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
542	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
543	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
544	  \[
545	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
546	  \]
547	\end{lemma}
548	\begin{proof}
549	
550	\leanok
551	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
552	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
553	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
554	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
555	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
556	\end{proof}
557	
558	\begin{lemma}
559	
560	\leanok
561	[The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix is the Cramer inverse]
562	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
563	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_I}
564	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
565	  The \(I\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
566	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the Cramer inverse:
567	  \[
568	    M_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}.
569	  \]
570	\end{lemma}
571	\begin{proof}
572	
573	\leanok
574	  By \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col} the \(I\)-column submatrix of \(M\) is
575	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\), and \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\)
576	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
577	\end{proof}
578	
579	\begin{lemma}
580	
581	\leanok
582	[\(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) realises the matrix formula]
583	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
584	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
585	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
586	  Applying the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
587	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) entrywise to the universal matrix \(X^J\) yields
588	  the image matrix:
589	  \[
590	    (\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast\, X^J \;=\; M \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
591	  \]
592	\end{lemma}
593	\begin{proof}
594	
595	\leanok
596	  On a free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\) (\(q \notin J\)) this holds by the definition of
597	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) as evaluation at the entries of \(M\)
598	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is
599	  an identity-block entry, which \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries to the
600	  corresponding entry of \(M_J = 1_{d\times d}\)
601	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{proof}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}
605	
606	\leanok
607	[Image of a cross minor under \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)]
608	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
609	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet}
610	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
611	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
612	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
613	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
614	  \[
615	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
616	  \]
617	\end{lemma}
618	\begin{proof}
619	
620	\leanok
621	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
622	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
623	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply
624	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}. Proved directly in Lean.
625	\end{proof}
626	
627	\begin{lemma}
628	
629	\leanok
630	[Naturality of the image matrix]
631	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
632	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
633	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
634	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
635	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
636	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
637	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
638	  \[
639	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
640	  \]
641	\end{lemma}
642	\begin{proof}
643	
644	\leanok
645	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
646	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
648	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
649	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
650	  Proved directly in Lean.
651	\end{proof}
652	
653	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
654	
655	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
656	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
657	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
658	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
659	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
660	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
661	\[
662	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
663	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
664	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
665	\]
666	
667	\begin{definition}
668	
669	\leanok
670	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
671	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
673	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
674	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
675	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
676	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
677	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
678	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
679	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
680	\end{definition}
681	
682	\begin{definition}
683	
684	\leanok
685	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
686	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
687	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
688	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
689	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
690	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
691	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
692	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
693	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
694	\end{definition}
695	
696	\begin{lemma}
697	
698	\leanok
699	[Left inclusion lies over \(R\)]
700	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
702	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_left}
703	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left})
704	  intertwines the two structure maps:
705	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/x]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
706	\end{lemma}
707	\begin{proof}
708	
709	\leanok
710	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
711	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
712	\end{proof}
713	
714	\begin{lemma}
715	
716	\leanok
717	[Right inclusion lies over \(R\)]
718	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
719	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}
720	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_right}
721	  The inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right})
722	  intertwines the two structure maps:
723	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/y]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
724	\end{lemma}
725	\begin{proof}
726	
727	\leanok
728	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
729	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
730	\end{proof}
731	
732	\begin{lemma}
733	
734	\leanok
735	[Left factor is a unit in the double localisation]
736	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
737	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}
738	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
739	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(x\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
740	\end{lemma}
741	\begin{proof}
742	
743	\leanok
744	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
745	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
746	  Proved directly in Lean.
747	\end{proof}
748	
749	\begin{lemma}
750	
751	\leanok
752	[Right factor is a unit in the double localisation]
753	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
754	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}
755	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
756	  For \(x, y \in R\), the image of \(y\) in \(R[1/(xy)]\) is a unit.
757	\end{lemma}
758	\begin{proof}
759	
760	\leanok
761	  \(xy\) is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\)
762	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}); a factor of a unit is a unit.
763	  Proved directly in Lean.
764	\end{proof}
765	
766	\begin{lemma}
767	
768	\leanok
769	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
770	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
772	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, def:gr_image_matrix}
773	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
774	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
775	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
776	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
777	  \[
778	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
779	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)
780	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot P^A_C ,
781	  \]
782	  a product of two units once \(P^A_C\) is inverted in \(D\).
783	\end{lemma}
784	\begin{proof}
785	
786	\leanok
787	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet},
788	  \(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C) = \det(M_C)\) with \(M = (X^A_B)^{-1} X^A\); split
789	  the \(C\)-minor of the product (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and the
790	  determinant as \(\det((X^A_B)^{-1}) \cdot P^A_C\). The first factor is a unit
791	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}); the second becomes a unit under
792	  \(\iota\) by hypothesis. Proved directly in Lean.
793	\end{proof}

795	\begin{definition}
796	
797	\leanok
798	[Localised transition map \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J \to S_I\)]
799	  \label{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}
800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleΘIJ}
801	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right, def:gr_minor_det}
802	  The triple-overlap transition map
803	  \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)] \to S_I = R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]\):
804	  the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) along the doubly
805	  inverted minor \(P^J_I P^J_K\) of the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
806	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) post-composed with the inclusion
807	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}\) into \(S_I\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}). It is
808	  well-defined because both inverted minors map to units of \(S_I\):
809	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\) by \cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit} (then
810	  \cref{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}), and \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K)\)
811	  by \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross} (with cross factor
812	  \(P^I_K\) a unit, \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_right}).
813	\end{definition}
814	
815	\begin{definition}
816	
817	\leanok
818	[Localised transition map \(\Theta_{J,K} : S_K \to S_J\)]
819	  \label{def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk}
820	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleΘJK}
821	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, def:gr_minor_det}
822	  The triple-overlap transition map
823	  \(\Theta_{J,K} : S_K = R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)] \to S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]\):
824	  the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
825	  \(\tilde\theta_{J,K}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) post-composed with the
826	  inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\) into \(S_J\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}),
827	  along \(P^K_I P^K_J\). Well-defined by \cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit} and
828	  \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross} (cross factor \(P^J_I\) a
829	  unit, \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}).
830	\end{definition}

832	\begin{definition}
833	
834	\leanok
835	[Localised transition map \(\Theta_{I,K} : S_K \to S_I\)]

838	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, def:gr_minor_det}
839	  The triple-overlap transition map
840	  \(\Theta_{I,K} : S_K = R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)] \to S_I = R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)]\):
841	  the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
842	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,K}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) post-composed with the
843	  inclusion \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\) into \(S_I\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}),
844	  along \(P^K_I P^K_J\). Well-defined by \cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit} and
845	  \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross} (cross factor \(P^I_J\) a
846	  unit, \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}).
847	\end{definition}
848	
849	\begin{lemma}
850	
851	\leanok
852	[Image-matrix identity over the triple overlap]
853	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
854	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
855	  \uses{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_universal_minor}
856	  Over the triple-overlap ring \(S_I\), the image matrix of \(\theta_{I,K}\)
857	  equals \(\Theta_{I,J}\) applied entrywise to the image matrix of
858	  \(\theta_{J,K}\):
859	  \[
860	    \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_\ast\bigl((X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\bigr)
861	      \;=\;
862	      \bigl(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\bigr)_\ast
863	        \bigl((X^J_K)^{-1} X^J\bigr) .
864	  \]
865	  Both sides reduce to \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\), where \(Y\) is the universal matrix
866	  \(X^I\) base-changed to \(S_I\) and \(Y_K\) its \(K\)-minor.
867	\end{lemma}
868	\begin{proof}
869	
870	\leanok
871	  The left side is \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\) by naturality of the image matrix
872	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}, via \cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}).
873	  For the right side, \(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\) carries the chart
874	  ring \(R^J\) over \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}\circ\tilde\theta_{I,J}\), so the image
875	  matrix of \(\theta_{J,K}\) maps to \((M^J_K)^{-1} M^J\) with
876	  \(M^J = (Y_J)^{-1} Y\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap},
877	  \cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}); the minor \(M^J_K = (Y_J)^{-1} Y_K\)
878	  (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) inverts via \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}\), and
879	  the cancellation \((Y_K)^{-1} Y_J \cdot (Y_J)^{-1} Y = (Y_K)^{-1} Y\)
880	  (\cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}, using \(\det Y_J\) a unit by
881	  \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}) gives \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\). Proved
882	  directly in Lean.
883	\end{proof}
884	
885	\begin{lemma}
886	
887	\leanok
888	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
889	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
890	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
891	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
892	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
893	  % affine patches" (read from
894	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
895	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
896	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
897	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
898	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
899	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
900	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
901	  %  is satisfied."
902	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
903	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
904	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
905	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
906	  \[
907	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
908	  \]
909	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
910	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
911	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
912	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
913	  glue the charts.
914	\end{lemma}
915	
916	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
917	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
918	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
919	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
920	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
921	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
922	% L838-L848.)
923	\begin{proof}
924	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
925	  \leanok
926	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
927	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
928	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
929	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
930	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
931	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
932	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
933	
934	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
935	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
936	  \[
937	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
938	  \]
939	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
940	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
941	  \[
942	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
943	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
944	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
945	  \]
946	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
947	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
948	  \[
949	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
950	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
951	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
952	  \]
953	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
954	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
955	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
956	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
957	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
958	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
959	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
960	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
961	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
962	\end{proof}
963	
964	\section{Scheme-level glue-data layer}
965	\label{sec:gr_glue_layer}
966	
967	The transition data of \cref{sec:gr_transition} lives at the level of rings. To
968	feed it to a scheme-gluing machine we must re-express each piece as a morphism in
969	the category of schemes: the charts \(U^I\), the principal-open overlaps
970	\(U^I_J\), their inclusions, and the transitions \(\theta_{I,J}\) all become
971	schemes and scheme morphisms by applying the contravariant functor \(\Spec\).
972	The lemmas of this section record those translations together with the two
973	genuinely geometric facts needed to assemble a glue datum: that each inclusion
974	\(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) is an open immersion, and that the fibre product of
975	two principal opens of \(\Spec A\) is again a principal open. They are
976	project-internal bridge results with no external source beyond the gluing
977	construction of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
978	
979	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the scheme-level layer}
980	
981	\begin{lemma}
982	[\(\Spec\) of an away-localisation is an open immersion]
983	  \label{lem:mathlib_specMap_localizationAway_isOpenImmersion}
984	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isOpenImmersion_SpecMap_localizationAway}
985	  \mathlibok
986	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
987	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in R\). The morphism
988	  \(\Spec R[1/f] \to \Spec R\) induced by the structure map \(R \to R[1/f]\) of
989	  the away-localisation is an open immersion (onto the basic open
990	  \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec R\)).
991	\end{lemma}
992	
993	\begin{lemma}
994	[Fibre product of two affine spectra is \(\Spec\) of the tensor product]
995	  \label{lem:mathlib_pullbackSpecIso}
996	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}
997	  \mathlibok
998	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
999	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(S\), \(T\) two \(R\)-algebras. The fibre
1000	  product of \(\Spec S \to \Spec R \leftarrow \Spec T\) is canonically isomorphic
1001	  to the spectrum of the tensor product:
1002	  \[
1003	    \Spec S \times_{\Spec R} \Spec T \;\cong\; \Spec\bigl(S \otimes_R T\bigr).
1004	  \]
1005	\end{lemma}
1006	
1007	\begin{lemma}
1008	[An away-localisation of a tensor product is a double away-localisation]
1009	  \label{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_away_mul}
1010	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul'}
1011	  \mathlibok
1012	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1013	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x, y \in R\), and \(R[1/x]\) the localisation
1014	  away from \(x\). Then \(R[1/x] \otimes_R R[1/y]\) is a localisation of \(R\)
1015	  away from \(x y\); equivalently it realises \(R[1/(xy)]\).
1016	\end{lemma}
1017	
1018	\begin{lemma}
1019	[Two away-localisations of the same element are canonically isomorphic]
1020	  \label{lem:mathlib_isLocalization_algEquiv}
1021	  \lean{IsLocalization.algEquiv}
1022	  \mathlibok
1023	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1024	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M \subseteq R\) a submonoid. Any two
1025	  localisations of \(R\) at \(M\) are uniquely \(R\)-algebra isomorphic; in
1026	  particular, given two rings each presented as \(R[1/x]\) there is a canonical
1027	  \(R\)-algebra isomorphism between them.
1028	\end{lemma}
1029

1033

1500	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1501	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1502	
1503	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1504	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1505	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1506	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1507	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1508	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1509	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1510	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1511	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1512	
1513	% ============================================================================
1514	% G1 decomposition (Stacks 01PB). The affine-local finiteness of the section
1515	% module splits into TWO halves:
1516	%   Half 1 — LOCALITY reduction (already proved in Lean): if the section module
1517	%     is finite on each member of a finite basic-open cover, it is finite on the
1518	%     affine. Blocks: lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1519	%     lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover.
1520	%   Half 2 — finite-type BASE CASE: on a globally-generated affine the section
1521	%     module is finite, via Gamma-exactness on affine quasi-coherent modules.
1522	%     Blocks (seams): lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated (seam 1),
1523	%     lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi (seam 2),
1524	%     lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated (base case = seam 2 + 3).
1525	% G1's proof then chains: cover-extraction (seam 1) + base case (Half 2) on each
1526	% member + locality glue (Half 1).
1527	% ============================================================================
1528	
1529	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1530	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1531	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1532	  \mathlibok
1533	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1534	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1535	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1536	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1537	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1538	\end{lemma}
1539	
1540	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1541	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1542	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1543	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1544	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1545	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1546	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1547	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1548	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1549	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1550	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1551	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1552	\end{lemma}
1553	
1554	\begin{proof}
1555	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1556	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1557	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1558	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1559	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1560	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1561	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1562	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1563	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1564	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1565	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1566	\end{proof}
1567	
1568	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1569	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
1570	  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1571	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1572	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1573	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1574	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
1575	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
1576	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1577	  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
1578	  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
1579	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
1580	  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
1581	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
1582	  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
1583	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1584	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1585	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1586	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1587	\end{lemma}
1588	
1589	\begin{proof}
1590	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1591	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1592	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1593	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1594	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1595	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1596	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1597	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1598	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1599	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1600	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1601	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1602	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1603	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1604	  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
1605	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1606	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1607	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1608	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1609	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1610	\end{proof}
1611	
1612	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
1613	  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1614	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1615	  % LEAN STATUS: base-case seam, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1616	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated; extracts a
1617	  % standard finite cover of W on whose members F is globally generated, from the
1618	  % SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType local-generators datum refined by quasi-compactness
1619	  % of the affine W.
1620	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1621	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
1622	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1623	  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
1624	  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
1625	  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
1626	  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
1627	  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1628	  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
1629	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1630	  cover of generated affines).}
1631	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules of finite type,
1632	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1633	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1634	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1635	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1636	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1637	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
1638	\end{lemma}
1639	
1640	\begin{proof}
1641	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1642	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering on each member of
1643	  which \(\F\) is generated by finitely many sections. Intersecting this covering
1644	  with the affine \(W\) and refining the resulting cover of \(W\) by standard basic
1645	  opens, every point of \(W\) lies in a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) on which
1646	  \(\F\) is generated by finitely many sections. Since \(W\) is affine, hence
1647	  quasi-compact, finitely many such basic opens \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already
1648	  cover \(W\); collect their defining elements into the finite family
1649	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\). That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1650	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly the condition
1651	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). A basic open of an affine is affine, and being
1652	  generated by a finite family of sections is precisely the existence of an
1653	  epimorphism from the corresponding finite free module. This is Stacks' passage
1654	  from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated affines.
1655	\end{proof}
1656	
1657	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1658	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1659	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction}
1660	  \mathlibok
1661	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}).}
1662	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1663	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1664	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1665	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1666	  this adjunction is the natural transformation
1667	  \(\Gamma(-)\,\widetilde{\phantom{x}} \to \mathrm{id}\) whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\)
1668	  is \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \top)} \to \mathcal{M}\), and
1669	  the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful.
1670	\end{lemma}
1671	
1672	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1673	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1674	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1675	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1676	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1677	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1678	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1679	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
1680	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1681	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1682	  % epi over an affine.
1683	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1684	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1685	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1686	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1687	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1688	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1689	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1690	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1691	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1692	  \(B\)-module map.
1693	\end{lemma}
1694	
1695	\begin{proof}
1696	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1697	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1698	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1699	  affine).}
1700	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1701	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1702	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1703	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1704	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1705	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1706	
1707	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1708	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1709	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1710	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1711	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1712	  \[
1713	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1714	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1715	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1716	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1717	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1718	  \]
1719	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1720	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1721	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1722	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1723	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1724	
1725	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1726	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1727	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1728	\end{proof}
1729	
1730	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1731	[Half 2 base case — a globally-generated finite-type qcoh sheaf has finite affine sections]
1732	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1733	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1734	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
1735	  % NOTE: (iter-047) prose-vs-Lean mismatch. The pinned Lean decl
1736	  % `gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated` is MORE GENERAL than this prose:
1737	  % it concludes finiteness from ANY qcoh epi `π : G ⟶ F` over Spec R with finite
1738	  % source sections `[Module.Finite R Γ(G)]` (not specifically the free epi
1739	  % `O_V^{⊕I} ↠ F` the prose describes). The free-epi case the prose states is the
1740	  % SEPARATE unpinned helper `gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_free_epi` (which also
1741	  % discharges the source-finiteness via the tilde-adjunction unit iso). Planner:
1742	  % either (a) generalise this prose to "any qcoh epi with finite source sections"
1743	  % to match the pin, OR (b) re-pin to `gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_free_epi` and add
1744	  % a block for the general form. The general Lean form is mathematically correct
1745	  % and subsumes the free-epi case (cf. iter-046 annihilator generalisation pattern).
1746	  % LEAN STATUS: base-case assembly, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1747	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated. This is seam 2
1748	  % (Gamma-epi) followed by seam 3 (free surjection ⟹ finite). It feeds Half 1 with
1749	  % each per-member Module.Finite Γ(X,D(g)) Γ(F,D(g)).
1750	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1751	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2094-L2107).
1752	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1753	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme. The quasi-coherent sheaf of
1754	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1755	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1756	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1757	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), write \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\), and let
1758	  \(\F\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules. Suppose \(\F\) is globally generated
1759	  by finitely many sections on \(V\): there is a finite index set \(I\) and an
1760	  epimorphism of \(\OO_V\)-modules \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_V\).
1761	  Then \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite \(B\)-module.
1762	\end{lemma}
1763	
1764	\begin{proof}
1765	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
1766	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB.}
1767	  Apply seam 2 (\cref{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}) to the epimorphism
1768	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_V\) over the affine \(V\): the section
1769	  functor yields a surjective \(B\)-module map
1770	  \(\Gamma(\OO_V^{\oplus I}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\). The source is the sections of a
1771	  finite free \(\OO_V\)-module, so \(\Gamma(\OO_V^{\oplus I}, V) \cong B^{\oplus I}\)
1772	  is a finite free \(B\)-module. A finite module surjecting onto \(\Gamma(\F, V)\)
1773	  forces \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) to be finite, by
1774	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}. Hence \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1775	  \(B\)-module.
1776	\end{proof}
1777	
1778	\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1779	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1780	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1781	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1782	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1783	        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1784	  % LEAN STATUS: G1 — project-built (mathlib-build target this iter); Mathlib has only
1785	  % the abstract SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType / IsQuasicoherent local-generator
1786	  % predicates, not this affine-local finiteness.
1787	  % NOTE: overlaps the QUOT keystone lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen — to be
1788	  % extracted to shared qcoh-affine-local infra (see STRATEGY Open questions).
1789	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1790	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1791	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1792	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1793	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1794	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1795	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1796	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1797	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1798	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1799	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1800	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1801	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1802	\end{lemma}
1803	
1804	\begin{proof}
1805	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover,
1806	        lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated,
1807	        lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1808	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1809	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1810	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1811	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1812	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\). Cover extraction
1813	  \cref{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated} gives a finite
1814	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) spanning the unit ideal, with each \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\))
1815	  affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) globally generated by finitely many sections. The base case
1816	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated} applied on each affine \(D(g)\)
1817	  makes \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). The locality reduction
1818	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover} then glues these per-member
1819	  finiteness statements to give \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1820	\end{proof}
1821	
1822	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1823	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1824	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1825	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1826	  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built; the geometric glue identifying Γ(F, W∩W_j)
1827	  % localisations across the source cover and the base-restriction is the missing
1828	  % plumbing. Stub-level this iter; built after G1.
1829	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1830	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1831	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1832	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1833	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1834	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1835	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1836	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1837	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1838	\end{lemma}
1839	
1840	\begin{proof}
1841	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1842	  Free modules are flat (\(\OO\)-flatness of a free module), so each \((M_j)_f\) is flat
1843	  over \(A_f\). Flatness of a module is local on the source: a module is flat over a
1844	  ring iff it is flat after localizing at every maximal ideal, and the family
1845	  \(\{W_j\}\) covers \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), so the per-patch flatness statements assemble to
1846	  flatness of the section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole
1847	  preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\). The flat locus is moreover stable under base-restriction along
1848	  the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\): for an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
1849	  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of
1850	  affines into a basic open being a localization), and for an affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
1851	  the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the section module
1852	  over the relevant patch \(W_j\) (via \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1853	  identifying the finite section modules and their localizations across the cover).
1854	  Flatness is preserved under localization of both the ring and the module, so
1855	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1856	\end{proof}
1857	
1858	
1859	\begin{theorem}
1860	
1861	
1862	
1863	\leanok
1864	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1865	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1866	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1867	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1868	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1869	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1870	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
1871	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
1872	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1873	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
1874	  %
1875	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
1876	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1877	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
1878	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
1879	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
1880	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
1881	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
1882	  %
1883	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (matches the landed .lean file):
1884	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1885	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] [QuasiCompact p] (F : X.Modules)
1886	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1887	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1888	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1889	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1890	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1891	  %
1892	  % NOTE (iter-023): `[QuasiCompact p]` is essential — LocallyOfFiniteType does NOT
1893	  % entail QuasiCompact; without it the statement is false (counterexample:
1894	  % ⊔_i Spec ℤ → Spec ℤ with a finite-type torsion sheaf). It is the extra clause
1895	  % promoting `LocallyOfFiniteType` to `FiniteType`, and there is no instance
1896	  % `LocallyOfFiniteType → QuasiCompact`. The header above carries it, and the Step-1
1897	  % proof prose below invokes it directly (no false "finite type ⟹ quasi-compact" step).
1898	
1899	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1900	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1901	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1902	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1903	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1904	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1905	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1906	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1907	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1908	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1909	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1910	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1911	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1912	
1913	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1914	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1915	\end{theorem}
1916	
1917	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1918	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1919	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1920	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1921	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1922	% by restricting to a non-empty
1923	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1924	\begin{proof}
1925	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1926	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1927	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1928	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1929	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1930	
1931	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1932	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1933	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1934	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1935	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1936	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1937	
1938	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1939	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1940	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1941	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1942	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1943	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1944	
1945	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1946	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1947	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1948	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1949	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1950	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1951	
1952	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1953	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1954	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1955	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1956	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1957	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1958	\end{proof}
1959	
1960	\section{Mathlib status}
1961	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1962	
1963	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1964	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1965	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1966	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1967	generic-flatness argument consumes
1968	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1969	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1970	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1971	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1972	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1973	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1974	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1975	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1976	are introduced in
1977	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
1978

content:
1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier
63	% to a 3-field structure
64	%     `structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where`
65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`
69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.
78	
79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  The structure morphism is the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
